topic: What intermittent fasting actually does to your body

slides.headline: Intermittent fasting doesn't actually burn more fat
slides.image_prompt: Cinematic medical visualization of human body cross-section showing adipose tissue and fat cells in warm amber tones, soft diffused lighting, photorealistic 3D rendering style with translucent layers revealing internal metabolism, professional medical textbook aesthetic, calming blue-grey background gradient

slides.headline: It just forces a calorie deficit
slides.subtext: Eating in a shorter window makes it harder to overeat. That's the real mechanism.
slides.image_prompt: Photorealistic overhead view of empty dinner plate with clock hands overlaid on table surface, soft natural window lighting, minimalist composition showing time-restricted eating concept, warm wood table texture, professional food photography style with medical precision, muted earthy color palette

slides.headline: Your metabolism doesn't magically speed up
slides.subtext: Studies show no metabolic advantage over regular calorie restriction with same total calories.
slides.image_prompt: Cinematic 3D medical illustration of human metabolic system with liver and mitochondria highlighted in soft golden glow, photorealistic cellular detail, cross-sectional view of organ tissue, professional medical visualization with depth and dimension, cool blue and warm amber color grading

slides.headline: But autophagy benefits ARE real
slides.subtext: After 12-16 hours fasted, cells start recycling damaged components. That's genuinely beneficial.
slides.image_prompt: Microscopic photorealistic visualization of cellular autophagy process, cells recycling internal components shown in soft blue and purple bioluminescent glow, detailed membrane structures, scientific microscopy aesthetic with bokeh effect, professional medical research photography style, peaceful and clean composition

slides.headline: It works if it helps YOU eat less
slides.subtext: Not magic. Just a tool that makes calorie control easier for some people.
slides.image_prompt: Warm cinematic scene of healthy person checking watch near kitchen window with morning light streaming in, soft focus background, photorealistic lifestyle medical photography, peaceful and contemplative mood, natural colors with slight desaturation, professional health documentary aesthetic

slides.headline: The best diet is one you'll actually follow
slides.subtext: Save this for later. Follow @[username] for evidence-based health insights.
slides.image_prompt: Soft-focus photorealistic image of open hands holding a glowing heart symbol representing sustainable health choices, warm golden hour lighting, shallow depth of field, professional medical lifestyle photography, hopeful and empowering composition, muted natural color palette with gentle warmth

caption:
I need to be honest with you about intermittent fasting.

The internet makes it sound like some kind of metabolic hack that rewires your fat-burning potential. I've had countless patients ask me if they're "missing out" by not doing it.

Here's what the research actually shows: intermittent fasting doesn't have a special fat-burning advantage over traditional calorie restriction when total calories are matched. The main reason it works for weight loss? It's simply harder to overeat when you're eating in a compressed window.

That said, the autophagy benefits after 12-16 hours are legitimate and worth considering. Your cells genuinely do start that cleanup process.

But the bigger truth? The best eating pattern is the one you can sustain long-term. For some people, that's IF. For others, it's regular meals. Both can be healthy.

Stop chasing the "perfect" diet. Start building habits you can actually live with.

Save this for later when someone tries to sell you on fasting being magic, and follow along for more evidence-based breakdowns that cut through the noise.
